instructions:
Build the Flutter analog of the TS doc-as-test framework. Same value model: extract ```dart code blocks from high-traffic docs, type-check against the real `amity_sdk` package using `dart analyze`, report pass/fail/skip. Goal: protect the Western customer cohort (social-heavy, Flutter-user-base) the same way TS doc-as-test protects Eastern.

New subdirectory: `.docs-ops/integration-tests/flutter/` (sibling to the TS framework, which stays at the integration-tests/ root). Future iOS / Android frameworks will get their own subdirs in the same pattern.

## Part A — Pubspec + analysis_options

`pubspec.yaml` declares amity_sdk as a local path dependency pointing at the sibling repo via `SP_SDKS_ROOT` (use the same env-var convention as the TS extractor — default to the docs repo's parent dir; document the override in README):

```yaml
name: docs_ops_flutter_tests
description: Type-check Flutter doc snippets against the real amity_sdk source.
environment:
  sdk: '>=3.0.0 <4.0.0'
dependencies:
  amity_sdk:
    path: ../../../../Amity-Social-Cloud-SDK-Flutter-Internal
```

(The path is relative to the pubspec's directory. From `.docs-ops/integration-tests/flutter/pubspec.yaml`, four `../` to reach `sp-sdks/`, then into the SDK repo.)

Verify: `dart pub get` in that directory should resolve cleanly. If the path doesn't work because of worktree weirdness, fall back to wrapping `dart pub get` with `SP_SDKS_ROOT` and constructing the path dynamically before each run.

`analysis_options.yaml` — strict analysis mode, but tolerate the unused-locals warnings that doc snippets routinely produce (e.g., a snippet showing `final foo = await ...` where `foo` is for show-only):

```yaml
include: package:lints/recommended.yaml
analyzer:
  errors:
    unused_local_variable: ignore
    unused_import: ignore
linter:
  rules: {}
```

## Part B — Preamble: wrap each block in a function body

Dart doesn't have a direct equivalent to TypeScript's `declare const`. The cleanest approach: wrap each extracted block in an `async` function that already has the context variables in scope.

`preamble.dart` — defines a single function shape that the extractor wraps each block with. Conceptually:

```dart
// preamble.dart — context for doc snippets. NOT used directly; the extractor
// wraps each extracted block in a function with these parameters in scope.
import 'package:amity_sdk/amity_sdk.dart';

Future<void> docSnippet({
  required String apiKey,
  required String userId,
  required String displayName,
  required String channelId,
  required String subChannelId,
  required String messageId,
  required String postId,
  required String communityId,
  required String commentId,
  required String pollId,
  required String fileId,
  required AmityChannel channel,
  required AmityMessage message,
  required AmityPost post,
  required AmityComment comment,
  required AmityCommunity community,
}) async {
  // <SNIPPET_BODY> (extractor inserts each block here)
}
```

Verify each named SDK type (`AmityChannel`, `AmityMessage`, etc.) exists in `.docs-ops/sdk-surface/flutter.json`. If a type doesn't exist (e.g., the Flutter SDK uses a different name), drop it from the preamble and note in your run report — be conservative; false declarations cause false negatives.

## Part C — Page list (`pages.json`)

Reuse the SAME high-traffic page set as the TS framework (`../pages.json` for reference). Pages without any `dart` blocks will produce zero output — that's fine. Add `flutter-quick-start.mdx` explicitly since it's the Flutter entry point even though it wasn't in the TS list.

Write out the full list (mirror the TS pages.json structure: chat_track + social_track + shared) plus the Flutter quickstart in a `flutter_specific` section. Resolve `enumerate:` entries at extract time.

## Part D — Block extractor (`extract-blocks.py`)

Mirror the TS extractor's structure. Differences:
  - Only extract blocks tagged ```dart (NOT typescript / ts / etc.).
  - For each block, generate a `.dart` file in `results/extracted/` with the preamble wrapper:

```dart
// source: <page>:<start>-<end>
import 'package:amity_sdk/amity_sdk.dart';

Future<void> docSnippet({
  required String apiKey,
  required String userId,
  // ... (same params as preamble.dart)
}) async {
  // <BLOCK_CONTENT>
}
```

  - The block's own `import` statements get hoisted to the TOP of the file (above the wrapper). If the block has imports, dedupe them against the wrapper's import.
  - Naming: same slug pattern as TS (`<page-slug>-block-<N>.dart`).
  - Honor `<!-- doc-as-test: skip -->` and `{/* doc-as-test: skip */}` markers exactly like the TS extractor does.

## Part E — Test runner (`run-tests.py`)

For each `.dart` file in `results/extracted/`:
  - Run `dart analyze <file>` (or `dart analyze --packages=.dart_tool/package_config.json <file>` if needed for the local-path dep)
  - Exit 0 + no errors emitted = PASS
  - Errors emitted = FAIL; capture them
  - Warnings emitted = still PASS but record warning count

Dart analyze error codes have names like `undefined_method`, `argument_type_not_assignable`, etc. — NOT the TSxxxx numeric codes. Adapt the gate policy from 0051 accordingly:

  - Errors of class `error` (severity: error) → BLOCKING (real type/API drift)
  - Errors of class `warning` (severity: warning) → WARNING (non-blocking)
  - Lints (info-level) → ignore

Emit `results/latest.json` with the same schema as TS doc-as-test, but include a `severity_by_error` count and use Dart-style error names instead of TS codes.

## Part F — README + .gitignore + first run

`README.md` — brief: what this is, how to run locally (`cd .docs-ops/integration-tests/flutter && dart pub get && python3 run-tests.py`), where the report lives.

Update top-level `.gitignore`: add `.docs-ops/integration-tests/flutter/results/extracted/*.dart` and `.docs-ops/integration-tests/flutter/.dart_tool/` and `.docs-ops/integration-tests/flutter/.packages` and `.docs-ops/integration-tests/flutter/pubspec.lock`.

RUN the framework end-to-end. Capture first-run `latest.json`. Expect 60-80% pass rate on first attempt (the TS framework hit 84%; Dart is a bit stricter on type inference so possibly lower). Don't try to fix failures in this task; report them in notes for the next round (mirror the 0048 → 0049 → 0050 iteration that worked for TS).

## Out of scope

- Wiring into `check-drift.py` — separate follow-up after the Flutter framework reaches >=95% pass+skip (same shape as TS gate-wiring in 0051).
- iOS / Android doc-as-test — separate per-platform tasks later.
- Fixing the Flutter SDK to add `dart` analyze setup — assume the SDK already builds; if not, flag in notes.

## Success criteria (LOOSE — framework build, not a quality gate yet)

- Framework runs end-to-end.
- `results/latest.json` is produced with the documented schema.
- At least 30 Dart blocks were extracted (sanity check that the pages were scanned and have Dart content).
- At least one block PASSES (sanity check that the toolchain works — if zero pass, the pubspec / preamble is wrong, not the docs).

Report in `notes`: stats from the first run; top 10 most common failure reasons (e.g., undefined_method vs argument_type_not_assignable); estimate of preamble gaps vs real doc drift; any pages with 0 dart blocks (just an FYI — those pages weren't authored to cover Flutter); any Flutter-specific quirks encountered (extension methods, named constructors, factory pattern, etc.).

validators.name: flutter_framework_runs_and_produces_valid_report
validators.command: test -f .docs-ops/integration-tests/flutter/results/latest.json && python3 -c "import json; d=json.load(open('.docs-ops/integration-tests/flutter/results/latest.json')); assert 'stats' in d and 'failures' in d; assert d['stats'].get('blocks_extracted',0)>=30, f'too few blocks: {d[\"stats\"].get(\"blocks_extracted\")}'; assert d['stats'].get('blocks_passed',0)>=1, 'zero blocks passed — toolchain likely broken'; print('OK')"
